title: fix: suppress stacktrace when snapshot validation fails
body:
Resolves #12692

### Problem

When YAML snapshots are missing `strategy` and/or `unique_key`, dbt shows a full Python stacktrace instead of just the clear error message. The `ValidationError` raised by `SnapshotConfig.final_validate()` is not a `DbtException`, so the CLI postflight decorator catches it as a `BaseException` and prints the traceback.

### Solution

Added a specific `ValidationError` catch in the `postflight` decorator (`core/dbt/cli/requires.py`) alongside the existing `DbtException` catch. Now users see just:

Encountered an error: Snapshots must be configured with a 'strategy' and 'unique_key'.


The traceback is still available via `logs/dbt_log.log` or `--debug`.

- [x] This PR includes tests, or tests are not required

title: fix: raise correct deprecation when config key is top-level in generic test (#12572)
body:
## Problem

Fixes #12572
Parent: #12394

When a user defines a config property like `where` at the top level of a generic test:
```yaml
data_tests:
  - unique:
      where: "valid_to is null"
```

dbt was raising `MissingArgumentsPropertyInGenericTestDeprecation`, which misleads the developer into moving `where` under `arguments:` — which is wrong. The correct fix is to move it under `config:`.

## Root Cause

In `TestBuilder.extract_test_args()`, the check for missing `arguments` property did not distinguish between actual test arguments and known config properties (`CONFIG_ARGS` like `where`, `severity`, `tags`, etc.). Any top-level key that wasn't `config`, `column_name`, `description`, or `name` triggered the wrong deprecation.

## Fix

1. **Exclude `CONFIG_ARGS` from the missing-arguments check** in `extract_test_args()` so that known config properties don't trigger `MissingArgumentsPropertyInGenericTestDeprecation`

2. **Emit `PropertyMovedToConfigDeprecation`** for each `CONFIG_ARGS` key found at the root level of the test definition, guiding the developer to move it under `config:`

## Test

Added `TestMissingArgsVsPropertyMovedToConfig` in `tests/functional/deprecations/test_deprecations.py` which verifies:
- `PropertyMovedToConfigDeprecation` fires for `where` at top level 
- `MissingArgumentsPropertyInGenericTestDeprecation` does NOT fire 

cc @dbeatty10
author: kalluripradeep

Thanks for this work !
Please address following comments and we can merge it !

title: Feature - Analyses Project Level Config
body:
Resolves #11427

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->

Project-level configuration for analyses in `dbt_project.yml` is silently ignored (#11427). Setting analyses: `+enabled: false` is accepted without error but has no effect. This is inconsistent with every other resource type (models, seeds, snapshots, exposures, etc.) which all support project-level config.

### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and
  tradeoffs you considered.
-->

This PR introduces a new behavior change flag `require_corrected_analysis_fqns` (default: False) that, when enabled:

1. Routes analysis config correctly — `NodeType.Analysis` in both `UnrenderedConfig` and `RenderedConfig` now reads from the analyses section of `dbt_project.yml`.
2. Removes the extra analysis FQN segment — `AnalysisParser.get_compiled_path()` returns `block.path.relative_path` directly (matching models, seeds, etc.) instead of prepending "analysis". This makes analysis FQNs consistent with other resource types (e.g. ['project', 'my_analysis']).
3. Adds analyses field to the `Project` class — The analyses config dict is now extracted from the project contract and passed through to RuntimeConfig.

When the flag is disabled (default), existing behavior is preserved — analyses use models config and retain the legacy FQN structure.

Gating behind a behavior flag ensures backward compatibility for anyone parsing manifest FQNs, while allowing users to opt into the corrected behavior.

title: fix: preserve case-insensitive env var lookup on Windows (#10422)
body:
Resolves #10422

### Problem

On Windows, environment variables are case-insensitive at the OS level. Since dbt 1.8, `env_var('local_user')` fails with `Env var required but not provided: 'local_user'` when the actual variable is set as `LOCAL_USER`.

The `preflight` function caches env vars via `get_env()` which returns `dict(os.environ)` — a plain case-sensitive Python dict. On Windows, `os.environ` is natively case-insensitive, but copying it into a regular dict loses that behavior. The `env_var()` Jinja function then looks up against this case-sensitive cache and fails when the casing doesn't match.

### Solution

Added a shared helper `_get_env_var(env, var)` in `base.py` that:
1. Tries `var in env` (the cached dict — exact case match)
2. If that fails and `os.name == "nt"`, falls back to `os.environ.get(var)` which preserves Windows' native case-insensitive lookup
3. Returns `(value, found_in_env)` tuple

Applied this helper to all 5 `env_var()` implementations across:
- `core/dbt/context/base.py` (BaseContext)
- `core/dbt/context/configured.py` (ConfiguredContext — schema YAML parsing)
- `core/dbt/context/secret.py` (SecretContext — profiles.yml / packages.yml)
- `core/dbt/context/providers.py` (ModelContext — model SQL parsing, TestContext — test SQL parsing)

### Checklist

title: Couple catalog loading into manifest loading
body:
Co-Authored-By:
  Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Resolves https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/12672

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->

`@requires.catalogs` and `@requires.manifest` were separate decorators that had to be applied independently to each CLI command. This created a footgun: any command that forgot `@requires.catalogs` would still parse and load the manifest, but catalog-level configs and model-level overrides (from catalogs.yml) would silently be missing — the manifest would resolve as if no catalogs were configured.

The underlying invariant is that any command loading a manifest expects catalog context to be present. Models that use catalog_name or rely on catalog-level configs with model-level overrides need catalog integration to be registered before the manifest is parsed in order for those configurations to resolve accurately.


### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and
  tradeoffs you considered.
-->

Move `load_catalogs()` directly into `setup_manifest()`, so catalog loading is always coupled to manifest loading. The `@requires.catalogs` decorator is removed entirely — there's no longer a way to load a manifest without first loading catalogs.

title: fix: handle null value in YAML to prevent TypeError during iteration
body:
<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->
When a source YAML file defines tables: with an explicit null value (or leaves it blank), dbt parse raises an unhandled `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable`. This happens because source.get("tables", []) returns None when the key is present but set to null — the default value is only used when the key is absent.

  Similarly, a null value for the top-level sources: key in a YAML file would cause the same issue. However, this is caught & handled in schema validation separately but I've put up a test for it as part of this work.

This was also an vulnerable pattern for exposure.tags iteration, and null packages iteration. These were both tested for and/or fixed as part of this work.

### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and
  tradeoffs you considered.
-->
 In core/dbt/parser/schemas.py, guard both iteration points against None:

  1. Changed contents.get("sources", []) to (contents.get("sources", []) or []) so a null sources: block is treated as an empty list.
  2. Added an explicit check for source.get("tables") is None and normalize it to [] before iterating, so a null tables: block is also treated as an empty list.

  A functional regression test (TestNullSourceTables) is included to prevent recurrence.

title: Fixed bug where Function schema wasnt available in on-run-end hook
body:
Resolves #12516 

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->

The schemas Jinja variable in on-run-end hooks does not include schemas created for UDF/function resources (#12516). When users iterate over schemas — e.g., to alter schema ownership — function schemas are silently skipped.

The root cause is in after_run, which builds the schemas set by filtering on `r.node.is_relational`. The `is_relational` property checks `self.resource_type in REFABLE_NODE_TYPES`, which only contains Model, Seed, and Snapshot. Since Function is not in that list, `FunctionNode.is_relational` returns False and its schema is excluded.

### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and
  tradeoffs you considered.
-->

Override `is_relational` on `FunctionNode` to return `True`. Functions map to objects in the database (which is what is_relational semantically means — "will this node map to an object in the database?"), so this is correct.

We intentionally do not add Function to `REFABLE_NODE_TYPES` because "refable" means "can be referenced with ref()" — functions use {{ function('name') }} instead. Overriding `is_relational` on `FunctionNode` decouples the two concepts.

title: improve independent default settings of sqlparse.MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS and MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH
body:
Resolves: https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/12694

 ## Problem

  The `--sqlparse` flag allows users to set `MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS` and `MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH` on the sqlparse engine to guard against performance issues with deeply
  nested or token-heavy SQL. However, the two settings were not independent: if a user set only one of them, the other would retain whatever value sqlparse had globally — either from a prior dbt invocation in the same process, or from sqlparse's own defaults.

  This meant that setting `{"MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS": "10000"}` could silently leave `MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH` at a restrictive value (or vice versa), causing unexpected compile failures for SQL that was previously unaffected.

  ## Solution

  Before applying user-supplied values, `SqlParseOptionsType.convert` now explicitly resets both `MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH` and `MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS` to `None`
  (sqlparse's permissive default) on every invocation. User-supplied values are then applied on top of these resets.

  This ensures the two settings are truly independent: configuring one has no side effect on the other, and each run starts from a clean, permissive baseline. Two new functional tests verify that setting only one option leaves the other as `None`.
author: MichelleArk

title: Fix EISDIR when snapshot name matches filename in multi-block file
body:
Resolves #

### Problem

PR #12568 enabled writing compiled SQL for snapshots during `dbt compile`. However, when a `.sql` file contains multiple snapshot blocks and one snapshot's name matches the filename, compilation fails with `[Errno 21] Is a directory`.

This happens because `get_target_write_path` (in `ParsedNode`) uses a basename heuristic to decide per-node whether to write a flat file or nest under a directory:

- Snapshot with matching name → `basename(path) == basename(original_file_path)` → writes a **file** at `snapshots/my_snap.sql`
- Snapshot with different name → basename mismatch → creates a **directory** at `snapshots/my_snap.sql/` and writes inside it

The OS cannot have both a file and a directory at the same path, so whichever runs second fails with EISDIR.

### Solution

Override `get_target_write_path` on `SnapshotNode` to always use the many-to-one (nested) path, skipping the basename heuristic. Since snapshot files can always contain multiple blocks, we should never assume a one-to-one relationship.

All snapshots now consistently nest under their source file:

```
target/compiled/project/snapshots/
├── my_snap.sql/
│   ├── my_snap.sql        # name matches filename — no conflict
│   └── other_snap.sql
└── snap.yml/
    └── yml_snapshot.sql
```

This also fixes a related issue where YML-based snapshot `compiled_path` values produced doubled paths (e.g., `snapshots/snap.yml/snapshots/snap.yml/name.sql`), addressed by an earlier change in `parser/schemas.py` (already on `main`).

title: Fix AttributeError when docs block argument is non-constant
body:
Resolves: N/A  

### Problem

  `_get_doc_blocks` crashed with an AttributeError when a` doc()` call in a description contained a non-literal Jinja2 argument — for example, a variable reference (`{{doc(my_variable) }}`) or a string concatenation expression (`{{doc('foo' ~ 'bar') }}`).

The root cause: the code iterated over all AST nodes in `node.args` and unconditionally accessed .value on each one. `.value` only exists on `jinja2.nodes.Const` (literal) nodes. Other jinja ast node types like `Name` (variable reference) or `Concat` (string concatenation) don't have this attribute, causing an unhandled `AttributeError`.

### Solution

 * Filter node.args to only include Const nodes before accessing .value:
  * Const is the only Jinja2 AST node type that is statically resolvable to a doc block name at parse time — the parser walks the AST without rendering the template, so variable references and expressions cannot be evaluated.
  * Non-Const args are silently skipped; the existing else: continue path handles the case where doc_args doesn't yield exactly 1 or 2 string values, meaning the doc() call is ignored for dependency tracking but does not crash. The description will stillrender correctly at runtime via normal Jinja2 evaluation.
author: MichelleArk

title: fix: inherit agg_time_dimension from semantic model defaults into simple metrics (TRIAGE-986)
body:
# Why

When using the v2 SL YAML syntax, setting `defaults.agg_time_dimension` on a semantic model should cause that value to be inherited by any simple metric that doesn't have its own explicit agg_time_dimension value.

However, it is not inherited by simple metrics. 

Fixes TRIAGE-986.

# What

In `_get_metric_type_params`, when building `MetricAggregationParams` for a v2 simple metric, fall back to the model-level `agg_time_dimension` when none is specified on the metric itself:

- Add `default_agg_time_dimension: Optional[str]` parameter to `parse_metric` and `_get_metric_type_params`
- In `parse_v2_metrics_from_dbt_model_patch`, pass `model_patch.agg_time_dimension` as the default — scoped to `MetricType.SIMPLE` only, matching the existing scoping of `generated_from`
- Apply fallback: `unparsed_metric.agg_time_dimension or default_agg_time_dimension`
- Update test assertions that incorrectly encoded the broken `null` behavior as expected

# Notes

- Explicit `agg_time_dimension` on a metric still takes precedence over the model default
- Only `MetricType.SIMPLE` metrics receive the default (cumulative, derived, ratio types are unaffected)
- The v1 SL YAML is unaffected — v1 measures always carry their own explicit `agg_time_dimension`

Drafted by claude-sonnet-4-6 under the direction of @theyostalservice

title: support custom ref kwargs in unit tests and generic data tests
body:
Resolves #12148

### Problem

The Core engine raises errors when unit tests or generic data tests use a custom `ref` macro override that accepts additional keyword arguments (e.g., `ref('stg_customers', revision=2)`). The Fusion engine handles this correctly.

Two root causes:

1. **Unit tests**: The static parser (`py_extract_from_source` from `dbt_extractor`) raises `ExtractionError` on unknown keyword arguments like `revision=2`, which gets converted to `InvalidUnitTestGivenInput`.

2. **Generic data tests**: The `TestContext` restricts available macros to only those in `depends_on.macros`. Custom `ref` macros are not in this restricted set, so the custom kwarg-to-version mapping never runs during parsing. At runtime the resolved ref doesn't match `depends_on.nodes`, causing `RefBadContextError`.

### Solution

Behind a new behavior change flag `support_custom_ref_kwargs` (default `False`):

1. **Unit tests** (`core/dbt/parser/unit_tests.py`): When `py_extract_from_source` fails with `ExtractionError`, fall back to Jinja rendering using the existing parse context. The custom `ref` macro executes, maps custom kwargs to standard ones, and the ref is captured via `builtins.ref()`.

2. **Generic data tests** (`core/dbt/context/providers.py`): Include user-defined `ref`/`source` macro overrides in the `TestContext` namespace so they're available during test kwarg rendering.

### Checklist

title: implementation of selector selector method
body:
Resolves #5009
Resolves #10992

### Problem
At present selectors can only be used by either setting default selector or passing them via `--selector` argument. This significantly limits the utility of selectors. We should be able to utilise selectors as just another _selector method_. This will allow us to cover [these](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/5009#issuecomment-3907705975) functionalities.

### Solution
Add a selector selector method class. This will require access to selector definitions and also for recursive calls to `get_selected` method from node selector. We pass these as extra arguments to `SelectorMethod` which will be ignored by existing methods but will be used by `SelectorSelectorMethod`. The selectors is passed from all `ResourceTypeSelector` so that this can be utilised upstream.

title: Fix #12353: Add @requires.catalogs decorator to compile command
body:
Fixes #12353

## Problem
The `dbt compile` command fails when using REST Catalog-Linked databases with the error:
```
Catalog not found.Received: MY_CATALOGExpected one of: INFO_SCHEMA, SNOWFLAKE?
```

## Root Cause
The `compile` function was missing the `@requires.catalogs` decorator, which is present on other commands that work with catalogs (`build`, `parse`, `run`, `seed`, `snapshot`).

## Solution
Added `@requires.catalogs` decorator to the `compile` function in `core/dbt/cli/main.py`.

This ensures catalogs are properly loaded before the compile task runs.

## Changes
- Added `@requires.catalogs` decorator to `compile` function
- Matches the pattern used by other catalog-aware commands

## Testing
As mentioned in the issue, the reporter confirmed that adding this decorator fixes the problem. The fix aligns with how other catalog-dependent commands are implemented.

## Related
- Suggested by @spcvalente in the issue description
- Aligns with existing patterns in the codebase

reviews.body:
We might need to gate this behind a behavior flag unfortunately, we should discuss with @graciegoheen

Historically we've avoided changing existing log lines whenever possible. That is because some customer's regex parse our logs messages (instead of ingesting the structured JSON logs). Said another way, some of our customers consider changes to common long standing log lines as breaking, because they've built automations / down stream services built on the log messages :melting_face::see_no_evil: Because of this, the current standard practice is to gate changes to existing log messages behind a behavior change flag. A good example is that this past year we added the event name to the message of all deprecation warnings, but we did so behind a behavior change flag ([example](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/64f631c27422b2bdc1f28432bd599e7b1e2effbf/core/dbt/events/types.py#L579)). However, I think over the past year we've moved more and more to the position of "We should not let people parsing our logs stop us from changing our logs", so maybe we can do this without a behavior change flag now, but should discuss it with @graciegoheen and come to a decision.

For others looking at the PR. A discussion happened offline and we decided to consider this kind of change safe for this PR and moving forward.

LGTM! 🚀

title: Fix list-type metric filters under models key rendered at parse time
body:
## Summary

When v2 metrics defined under the `models:` key use **list-type filters**, the YAML schema renderer incorrectly attempts to render them at parse time, causing `'Dimension' is undefined` errors.

### The bug

`SchemaYamlRenderer.should_render_keypath()` uses `deep_map_render` from `dbt_common`, which builds a keypath tuple as it traverses nested structures. For **dict keys**, it appends strings (e.g. `"filter"`). For **list elements**, it appends integer indices (e.g. `0`, `1`).

The `models` key handler checked `keypath[-1] == "filter"`, which works for string filters where the keypath looks like `('metrics', 0, 'filter')`. But for list filters like:

```yaml
filter:
  - "{{ Dimension('entity__is_fraud') }} = false"
  - "{{ Dimension('entity__is_employee') }} = false"
```

...the keypath for each element is `('metrics', 0, 'filter', 0)` — and `keypath[-1]` is `0` (int), not `"filter"`. So the check fails, the renderer tries to evaluate the Jinja, and `Dimension` is not in the parse-time context.

### The fix

One-line change in `core/dbt/parser/schema_renderer.py`:

```python
# Before (only catches string filters):
if keypath[-1] == "filter" and "metrics" in keypath:

# After (catches both string and list filter elements):
if "filter" in keypath and "metrics" in keypath:
```

This matches the approach already used by the `metrics` key handler (line 107), which checks `keypath[-1] == "filter" or keypath[-2] == "filter"` to handle both forms.

### New tests

**General coverage tests** (these all passed before the fix — they fill gaps in test coverage for list-type filters and nested filter locations that previously had no unit tests):

- `test__metrics_list_filter` — list-type top-level filters for standalone `metrics` key
- `test__metrics_nested_filter` — `type_params.numerator.filter` as both string and list for standalone `metrics` key
- `test__models_v2_metric_filter_string` — string filter under `models` key (regression test)
- `test__models_v2_nested_metric_filters` — `numerator.filter`, `input_metrics[].filter`, `base_metric.filter` as string and list under `models` key

**Bug-specific regression test** (this test **fails without the fix** with `'Dimension' is undefined`):

- `test__models_v2_metric_filter_list` — list-type filter on v2 metrics under the `models` key

## Test plan

- [x] All 10 unit tests pass: `python -m pytest tests/unit/parser/test_schema_renderer.py -v`
- [x] `test__models_v2_metric_filter_list` fails without the fix (verified by reverting the one-line change)
- [x] `test__models_v2_nested_metric_filters` also fails without the fix for list-form nested filters
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (black, flake8, mypy, isort)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: theyostalservice

commits.message:
Fix list-type metric filters under models key rendered at parse time

When v2 metrics defined under the `models:` key use list-type filters
(e.g. `filter: ["{{ Dimension(...) }}", "{{ Dimension(...) }}"]`), the
`deep_map_render` function appends integer indices to the keypath for
list elements. The previous check `keypath[-1] == "filter"` only matched
string filters (where the last element is literally "filter"), but missed
list elements where `keypath[-1]` is an integer like 0 or 1.

Changed to `"filter" in keypath` which correctly matches both forms.

Tighten metric filter check to use positional keypath matching

Use keypath[0] == "metrics" and keypath[-1]/[-2] == "filter" instead of
membership checks, to be consistent with the standalone "metrics" branch
and avoid over-matching on non-terminal or non-root keys.

title: Write compiled SQL for snapshots during dbt compile
body:
Resolves #7867

### Problem

`dbt compile` writes compiled SQL to `target/compiled/` for models, tests, and analyses — but not for snapshots. Interactive compile (`dbt compile --select <snapshot>`) does produce output, creating an inconsistency. This has been the case since snapshots were called "archives" and defined in `dbt_project.yml`, where there was no file to write to. Now that snapshots are defined in SQL files, this limitation no longer makes sense.

Additionally, multiple snapshots can be defined in the same file via `{% snapshot %}` blocks, and they all shared the same `compiled_path`, which would cause collisions if writing were enabled.

### Solution

1. **Remove `NodeType.Snapshot` from the disallowlist in `_write_node`** (`compilation.py`) so snapshot compiled SQL is written to `target/compiled/`.
2. **Change `get_compiled_path`** (`parser/snapshots.py`) to return the snapshot block name (`block.name + ".sql"`) instead of the file's relative path, ensuring each snapshot gets a unique output path.
3. **Override `parse_node`** in `SnapshotParser` to decouple FQN computation from the compiled path — FQN still uses the original file path for backward compatibility.

Output layout:
- Single snapshot per file (name matches filename): `target/compiled/<project>/snapshots/<name>.sql`
- Multiple snapshots per file: `target/compiled/<project>/snapshots/<file>.sql/<name>.sql` (same pattern used for schema tests under `schema.yml/`)

title: Add an AGENTS.md file to the project
body:
We're seeing an increase in PRs overall, and a lot are either entirely or partially created using LLMs. Generally, I think that is a good thing. People having easier access to fix bugs and add features is cool. However, we need to balance that with stability and long term maintainability of the project. Some of the reoccurring issues we're seeing are things like:
* Testing best practices specific to the project (generally one test per functional test class)
* Commit hygiene (lack of this makes it harder to review)
* New code ending up in the wrong area of the code base

Adding an AGENTS.md file should hopefully improve these types of problems. In turn this should allow us to review and ship the flood of PRs more easily maintaining stability and lessening the long-term maintainability burden.
author: QMalcolm

commits.message:
Add an AGENTS.md markdown file

We're seeing an increase PRs that were either entirely or partially
created using LLMs. Adding an AGENTS.md file should hopefully aid in
the quality of PRs submitted.

Add a `Commit Discipline` section to AGENTS.md

One common thing I've run up against when reviewing OSS Community PRs
is that the work tends to happen in one large commit, or as few large
commits. These large commits generally mix things like cleanup,
abstraction, bug fixes, feature adds, and refactors. Mixing these makes
it harder to review PRs. Asking the agent to follow commit disciple
should make it _easier_ to review PRs which in turn will help with
stability and long term maintainability.

Add context on functional test flakiness to AGENTS.md

Our testing setup for our functional tests means that multiple tests
in the same test class share the same underlying dbt project files.
This is unfortunate in that multiple tests in the same functional test
class can lead to flakiness is one test modifies the underlying dbt
project. Adding this context to the AGENTS.md should hopefully mean we
get fewer flaky tests moving forward.

title: Fix duplicate CTE race condition in ephemeral model compilation
body:
Resolves https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/12619

## Summary

- Adds a per-node `threading.Lock` to `CompiledResource` to eliminate TOCTOU race conditions when multiple threads compile nodes that `ref()` the same ephemeral model
- Wraps the two critical sections in `_recursively_prepend_ctes` (ephemeral compilation and CTE injection) with the ephemeral/consuming node's lock
- Makes `set_cte` thread-safe to prevent duplicate CTE appends
- Per-node lock granularity preserves parallelism — threads only block when competing for the exact same ephemeral node

## Test plan

- [x] Added `tests/unit/test_compilation_threading.py` with 4 regression tests:
  - Concurrent `set_cte` deduplication (20 threads, assert single CTE)
  - Concurrent ephemeral compilation (assert compiled exactly once)
  - Lock excluded from serialization
  - Lock restored after deserialization round-trip
- [x] All 61 existing `tests/unit/contracts/graph/test_nodes.py` tests pass
- [x] All code quality checks pass (`hatch run pre-commit run --all-files`)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: colin-rogers-dbt

commits.message:
Fix duplicate CTE race condition in ephemeral model compilation

Add per-node threading.Lock to CompiledResource to prevent duplicate CTEs
when multiple threads compile nodes that ref() the same ephemeral model.
The existing check-then-act patterns in _recursively_prepend_ctes and
set_cte were classic TOCTOU races causing duplicate CTEs ~1/50 runs.

Per-node lock granularity preserves parallelism — threads only block when
competing for the exact same ephemeral node. No deadlock risk since
ephemeral deps form a DAG and lock acquisition follows dependency order.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add pickle support for per-node threading.Lock

Add __getstate__/__setstate__ to CompiledResource so the _lock field
is excluded during pickling and recreated on unpickle, fixing
TypeError: cannot pickle '_thread.lock' object.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Move _lock from CompiledResource to CompiledNode per review

Address PR feedback: define _lock on the runtime CompiledNode class
instead of the serialized CompiledResource. This avoids all mashumaro
serialization concerns since extra attributes on CompiledNode are
automatically dropped during serialization. Also restores the dropped
comment explaining the check-then-act pattern, and removes the
unnecessary lock from set_cte (only called with sql=None during parsing).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Avoid deadlock: narrow lock scope and recurse outside lock

Move the recursive _recursively_prepend_ctes call outside the
cte_model._lock scope. The old structure would deadlock with
threading.Lock (non-reentrant) because the recursive call re-acquires
the same node's lock for CTE injection. Now the lock only covers the
compile-or-skip decision, and recursion happens after release.

Add test that validates the lock acquisition pattern doesn't deadlock
by simulating the exact sequence from _recursively_prepend_ctes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add explicit check for unset new_prepended_ctes

Initialize new_prepended_ctes to None and raise DbtInternalError if
it's still None after the lock/recursion branches. This replaces a
potential cryptic UnboundLocalError with a clear diagnostic message
if a future code change breaks the branch coverage.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: support sql_header config for data tests
body:
Resolves #9775

### Problem

The `sql_header` config is not supported for data tests (singular and generic). When users configure `sql_header` — either via the `set_sql_header` macro in singular tests or via YAML `config:` in generic tests — the value is silently dropped during parsing because:

1. `TestConfig` has no `sql_header` field, so any value set during parsing is discarded during serialization.
2. `TestBuilder.CONFIG_ARGS` does not include `sql_header`, so generic tests don't recognize it as a config key from YAML and it gets treated as a test argument instead.

This prevents users from prepending SQL statements before test execution — a capability that already works for models.

### Solution

**Add `sql_header` as a recognized config field for data tests:**

- Add `sql_header: Optional[str] = None` to `TestConfig` dataclass and include it in the `same_contents` modifiers list for partial parsing support.
- Add `"sql_header"` to `TestBuilder.CONFIG_ARGS` so generic tests accept `sql_header` in their YAML config block.

**Note:** A companion PR in [dbt-adapters](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-adapters) is needed to actually prepend the `sql_header` in the test and unit test materialization macros (`test.sql` and `unit.sql`). Without that change, the config value is correctly parsed and stored but not applied during execution.

title: Reduce test duration run time
body:
### Problem

The `update-test-durations.yml workflow` ran all ~1,367 integration tests in a single job, taking over 5 hours to complete. This was:
1. Slow: 6-hour timeout was often hit
2. Inaccurate: Tests competing for resources inflated duration measurements (e.g., a test showing 76s when it actually takes 1.6s in CI)

### Solution

Restructured the workflow to match how main.yml runs tests:
- 15 parallel matrix jobs - Each runs ~90 tests using --splits 15 --group N
- Merge step - Downloads all partial .test_durations_N files and combines them
- No coverage - Removed --cov since we only need timing data, not coverage reports
- Aligned hatch script - New integration-tests-generate-durations script matches integration-tests config (same splitting algorithm, durations path)

title: Fix Docker build failure caused by apt-get dist-upgrade conflicting with version-pinned packages
body:
## Problem

The Docker release build started failing with `exit code: 100` because `apt-get dist-upgrade -y` was upgrading packages (e.g., `ca-certificates` from `20210119` to `20230311`) before `apt-get install` could install the version-pinned packages. This worked for years because the pinned versions matched what was available in the Debian bullseye repositories, but recent repository updates introduced newer package versions that `dist-upgrade` now picks up, causing the conflict.

## Solution

Split the apt package installation into two phases:
1. Install version-pinned packages first, then use `apt-mark hold` to protect them from upgrades
2. Run `dist-upgrade` to apply security updates to non-pinned base image packages
3. Install temporary build dependencies (unpinned, since they get removed after building git)

This preserves reproducible builds via version pinning while still allowing security updates for other packages.

## Testing

- Tested the Docker build locally with `docker build -f docker/Dockerfile --target dbt-core .`

Merge source freshness from 5 locations (in ascending order of precedence):

5. `table` config

Currently, partial parsing is invalidated per-file if there is a whitespace-only addition to .sql files - requiring the file to be fully re-parsed. 


### Solution

normalize checksums by compacting whitespace and newlines to a single space. this normalization is not applied to seed .csv files since the checksum also powers state:modified comparisons and would lead to initial false positives compared to prior versions.

reviews.body:
I wasn't sure how I felt about this at first. It _is_ a nice optimization. The concern in the back of my mind is that when white space does matter. For instance, if two valid SQL words with the whitespace removed became a third valid SQL word. Something like `ON TO` become `ONTO` (I don't think that's a real example, but a hypothetical example). I think that the edge case is unlikely enough that this _should_ be safe. We'd have to do a full analysis of SQL dialect words 🙃 

An alternative (that I don't think we should do) would be to write a regex so that we replace any instances of a series of white space with a single space. However, the time complexity of regex on every file might negate any gains we get by avoiding unnecessary parsing.

title: Fix: allow macros invoked via run-operation to ref() private and protected models
body:
Resolves #8248

### Problem

When executing `dbt run-operation` with a macro that calls `ref()` on a private model, dbt raises a `DbtReferenceError`:

```
Node macro.my_project.my_macro attempted to reference node model.my_project.private_model,
which is not allowed because the referenced node is private to the 'analytics' group.
```

The access validation methods `is_invalid_private_ref()` and `is_invalid_protected_ref()` in `manifest.py` already exclude `NodeType.SqlOperation` and `NodeType.RPCCall` from group checks (since those represent ad-hoc preview queries), but they do not exclude `NodeType.Macro`. Since macros are outside the group/access control system and don't have a `group` attribute, they should be permitted to reference any model.

### Solution

Add `NodeType.Macro` to the exclusion list in both `is_invalid_private_ref()` and `is_invalid_protected_ref()`, alongside the existing `SqlOperation` and `RPCCall` exclusions. This follows the same pattern already established for other node types that operate outside the group/access framework.

**Changes:**
- `core/dbt/contracts/graph/manifest.py` — add `and node.resource_type != NodeType.Macro` to both validation methods
- `tests/functional/run_operations/` — add regression test (`TestRunOperationRefPrivateModel`) that materializes a private model and verifies `run-operation` can `ref()` it successfully

title: Support for declaraing variables in a vars.yml file
body:
Resolves #11144
Resolves #2955
Resolves https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/4873

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->

Project variables must currently be defined within `dbt_project.yml`, which becomes unwieldy for projects with many variables. Additionally, variables cannot be referenced within `dbt_project.yml` itself at parse-time [ requires `--vars` CLI arg ], limiting configuration flexibility.

### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and
  tradeoffs you considered.
-->

Introduce support for a dedicated `vars.yml` file at the project root that is parsed before `dbt_project.yml`. This enables:

  - Separation of concerns: Variables live in their own file, keeping `dbt_project.yml` cleaner
  - Parse-time variable references: Variables from `vars.yml` can be used in `dbt_project.yml` via `{{ var('...') }}`
  
### Testing Checklist
[x] - `dbt_project.yml` with var should be rendered properly when var is set through vars.yml.
[x] - `dbt_project.yml` with var should be rendered properly when var is set through cli
[x] - `dbt_project.yml` with var should be rendered properly with CLI vars when set through both vars.yml and cli
[x] - dbt should throw error if `dbt_project.yml` expects a var not present in `vars.yml`
[x] - SQL model with variable should be rendered properly when var is set through vars.yml
[x] - SQL model with variable should be rendered properly when var is set through cli
[x] - SQL model with variable should be rendered properly with CLI vars when set through both vars.yml and cli
[x] - dbt should throw an error if sql model expects a var not set in vars.yml
[x] - Macro using var should be rendered properly when var is set through vars.yml
[x] - Macro using var should be rendered properly when var is set through cli
[x] - Macro using var should be rendered properly with CLI vars when set through both vars.yml and cli
[x] - Variable expected in `dbt_project.yml` should throw an error if that variable is only present in `dbt_project.yml` ( We only support dbt_project.yml rendering when var is declared in `vars.yml` or CLI `--vars` )
[x] - Project should throw error when variables are set in both vars.yml and dbt_project.yml
[x] - If `vars.yml` file is empty, vars from `dbt_project.yml` should be used.
[x] - Vars declared in `vars.yml` without a top level `vars` key should use vars from CLI or `dbt_project.yml`
[x] - PartialParsing should be disabled if any variable has changed

I am seeing the same unit test failures on my local check out of this branch as are being raised in CI. The failures seem to come in two forms

```
  ERROR <test-path> - TypeError: Project.__init__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'vars_from_file'
```

It looks like we just have some additional mocking to do to get CI to pass 🙂 To solve the 80 `ERROR` tests, I believe we just need to add `vars_file_dict={},` to the `project` fixture in [tests/unit/utils/project.py](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/9cf32c8ab3022732d023a95afacc9844831489b7/tests/unit/utils/project.py#L29). Reading through this PR such a test should pass (i.e. CLI vars are referenced), but we should ensure we assert that in our tests

title: Support unit testing models that depend on source with the same name
body:
Resolves https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11975
Resolves https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/10433

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->
Currently, it is not possible write a unit test against a model that depends on multiple sources with the same name. This is for a couple reasons:
1. The unit test manifest does not consider the source schema name as part of the unique id for the 'dummy' model inserted, and so sources with the same names will get clobbered
2. Duplicate CTEs (same names) are created past that point because CTE names are based on identifier name

### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and

-->

title: Fix #12379: Fixed Column Type Validation Logic and added tests for th…
body:
Resolves #12379

### Problem

Currently, [dbt](cci:1://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/core/dbt/context/providers.py:1133:4-1135:34) does not explicitly validate if the columns specified in `config.column_types` for a seed actually exist in the CSV file. If a user defines a type for a non-existent column, it might cause confusion or be silently ignored by the underlying library without clear feedback. This PR aims to improve the developer experience by validating these inputs and providing helpful warnings.

### Solution

This PR implements validation logic to ensuring that [column_types](cci:1://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:128:4-146:37) specified in the seed configuration are present in the actual CSV file.

**Implementation Details:**

*   **Fixed Column Type Validation Logic ([dbt-core/core/dbt/context/providers.py](cci:7://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/core/dbt/context/providers.py:0:0-0:0))**:
    *   Modified the [load_agate_table()](cci:1://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/core/dbt/context/providers.py:1253:4-1308:20) method to validate column existence before passing [column_types](cci:1://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:128:4-146:37) to agate.
    *   Reads the CSV file header to identify available columns.
    *   Checks if each column defined in [column_types](cci:1://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:128:4-146:37) config exists in the header.
    *   Emits a [dbt-core](cci:7://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core:0:0-0:0) `JinjaLogWarning` for any invalid columns with clear context.
    *   Removes invalid columns from the [column_types](cci:1://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:128:4-146:37) dictionary passed to agate to prevent downstream issues.
    *   Handles edge cases such as file read errors, empty files, or invalid delimiters.

*   **Warning Message Format**:
    When a column type is specified for a non-existent column, [dbt-core](cci:7://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core:0:0-0:0) now emits:
    > Column type specified for non-existent column '{column_name}' in seed '{seed_name}' (file: {path}). This column type override will be ignored.

*   **Comprehensive Tests ([dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py](cci:7://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:0:0-0:0))**:

    *   [TestSeedColumnTypesBasic](cci:2://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:113:0-146:37): Tests basic seed column types functionality to ensure no regressions.
    *   [TestSeedColumnTypesWithMismatchedColumns](cci:2://file:///e:/Personal%20Projects/OpenSource/dbt/dbt-core/tests/functional/seeds/test_seed_column_type_validation.py:149:0-201:59): Tests seeds where column types reference non-existent CSV columns.

*   [x] Emits dbt-core warning with clear context (seed name, file path, invalid column name)
*   [x] Excludes invalid columns from column types passed to agate

title: Fix doc() rendering in derived_semantics descriptions
body:
## Summary

- `_is_norender_key` in `schema_renderer.py` handles depth-3 keypaths like `(columns, 0, description)` to skip Jinja rendering of descriptions during YAML parsing
- But `derived_semantics` adds an extra nesting level: `(derived_semantics, dimensions, 0, description)` at depth 4
- Without this fix, `{{ doc(...) }}` in `derived_semantics.dimensions[].description` or `derived_semantics.entities[].description` gets rendered during YAML parsing before `doc()` is available, causing `'doc' is undefined` errors

Related to #12529 (same area of code — metric filter rendering)

title: fix dbt retry for microbatch models
body:
Resolves #11423

### Problem

When a microbatch model fails during `dbt run` and `dbt retry` is executed later (e.g., days after), the retry uses the **current date** instead of the **original failure date** to compute which batches to run. This results in processing completely different batches than those that originally failed.

**Root cause:** In `retry.py`, the `batch_map` is only populated when a microbatch model had **both** successful and failed batches (`len(result.batch_results.successful) != 0`). When **all** batches fail (or the model was entirely skipped due to an upstream failure), the model is excluded from `batch_map`, so `previous_batch_results` is never set on the node. This causes `get_batches()` in `run.py` to fall through to normal batch computation, which uses `get_invocation_started_at()` — the current retry time, not the original run time.

**Example from the issue:** A model failed on 2025-03-21 with batches 03-18 through 03-21. When retried on 2025-03-25, it processed batches 03-22 through 03-25 instead — completely missing the original failed batches.

### Solution

Track the original invocation time from the `run_results.json` artifact metadata and use it during batch computation on retry.

**Changes:**

1. **`core/dbt/task/retry.py`**: Extract `invocation_started_at` from the previous run's metadata and pass it to the `RunTask` as `original_invocation_started_at`. Also changed `self.task_class == RunTask` to `issubclass(self.task_class, RunTask)` so that `BuildTask` (which extends `RunTask`) also gets microbatch retry behavior.

2. **`core/dbt/task/run.py`**: Added `original_invocation_started_at` attribute to `RunTask`. Updated `MicrobatchModelRunner.get_microbatch_builder()` to use `self.parent_task.original_invocation_started_at` as `default_end_time` when available, falling back to `get_invocation_started_at()` for normal (non-retry) runs.

3. **`tests/unit/task/test_run.py`**: Added two tests verifying that the microbatch builder uses the original invocation time during retry and falls back to the current time during normal runs.

title: Fix v2 metric parsing to respect custom semantic_model.name
body:
## Problem

When a v2 semantic model YAML uses a custom `semantic_model.name` override (e.g., `semantic_model: { name: custom_name }`), the semantic model is correctly registered in the manifest under the custom name. However, `parse_v2_metrics_from_dbt_model_patch` always uses `model_patch.name` (the dbt model name) as `generated_from` for simple metrics, ignoring the custom name.

This causes MetricFlow validation to fail for conversion metrics with:
```
Input metric 'my_simple_metric' for conversion metric 'my_conversion_metric'
is linked to a semantic model that does not exist in your manifest.
```

The simple metric's `metric_aggregation_params.semantic_model` is set to the dbt model name (e.g., `fct_orders`), but the manifest only contains the semantic model under the custom name (e.g., `fct_orders_semantic_model`).

## Fix

Resolve the semantic model name respecting custom `semantic_model.name` overrides in `parse_v2_metrics_from_dbt_model_patch`, mirroring the same name-resolution logic already used in `parse_v2_semantic_model_from_dbt_model_patch` (lines 1041-1045).

## Tests

- `TestMetricOnModelWithCustomSemanticModelName` -- verifies simple metrics reference the custom semantic model name and conversion metric validation passes
- `TestMetricOnModelWithoutCustomSemanticModelName` -- verifies simple metrics reference the dbt model name when no custom name is set (regression guard)

title: Check installed packages exist based on locked deps
body:
Resolves #12509

### Problem
To check if a package has been uninstalled we currently check if the count of installed packages >= count of packages defined in packages.yml. This logic is incorrect because if a package has `n` nested dependencies then even if `n` random package get removed from `dbt_packages` dir the check will still still pass and not raise any errors.

### Solution
Instead of comparing package counts from packages.yml we will compare the actual package names from `package-lock.yml` with the installed package's directory names and check if there are any locked packages that are not installed.

title: Fix: set expr on column-based dimensions/entities when name differs from column
body:
## Summary
- When using v2 semantic layer YAML with column-level dimension or entity definitions, if the `name` differs from the column `name`, `expr` was not being set in the semantic manifest
- This caused MetricFlow to generate SQL using the dimension/entity name instead of the actual warehouse column name, resulting in `invalid identifier` errors during `dbt sl validate`
- Sets `expr` to the column name when the dimension/entity name differs from the column name, matching the behavior already implemented in dbt Fusion (`resolve_semantic_models.rs`)

Fixes #12512

## Example

```yaml
columns:
  - name: afe_number           # actual warehouse column
    dimension:
      type: categorical
      name: approval_afe_number  # user-friendly dimension name
```

**Before:** `Dimension(name="approval_afe_number", expr=None)` → MetricFlow queries `APPROVAL_AFE_NUMBER` → error
**After:** `Dimension(name="approval_afe_number", expr="afe_number")` → MetricFlow queries `AFE_NUMBER` → works

## Test plan
- [x] Added unit tests for `_parse_v2_column_dimensions` and `_parse_v2_column_entities` covering name override, name match, empty name, and shorthand cases
- [x] Added `expr` assertions to existing integration test `TestSemanticModelParsingWorks`
- [x] All existing parser unit tests pass
author: b-per

reviews.body: Thanks for doing this!

commits.message:
Fix FK constraint ref() resolving to deferred relation when target is selected

During deferral, foreign key constraint ref() was always resolving to the
deferred (production) relation even when the referenced model was being
built as part of the current selection. This caused FK constraints to
reference the wrong schema.

The fix adds selection awareness to FK constraint compilation:
- Track selected node IDs on the Compiler instance
- Use deferred relation only if FK target is NOT in selection
- Propagate selection info from task to runner compilers

This mirrors the behavior of RuntimeRefResolver.create_relation() for
model body refs, ensuring FK constraints behave consistently.

Fixes #12455

Set expr on column-based dimensions and entities when name differs from column (#12512)

When using v2 semantic layer YAML with column-level dimension/entity
definitions, if the name differs from the column name, expr was not set
in the manifest. This caused MetricFlow to query the dimension/entity
name instead of the actual warehouse column, resulting in invalid
identifier errors during `dbt sl validate`.

title: Enforce dimensions have 'validity_params' only if they have granularity
body:
Resolves [DI-3180](https://dbtlabs.atlassian.net/browse/DI-3180)

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

~DEPENDS ON #12472~ Updated: upstream PR is merged

### Problem

Time granularity values are required for a dimension to have a valid validity_params value.  (This is extra, extra true as, due to some v1 YAML choices, it's a baked-in typing requirement when constructing [DimensionTypeParams](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-semantic-interfaces/blob/main/dbt_semantic_interfaces/protocols/dimension.py#L41), which are the container object that will also contain the validity params.)  
  
Since they're required, this doesn't functionally change what behaviors are available to users, but it does provide drastically clearer errors that reflect the actual YAML they are parsing. 

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->

### Solution

If you'd like, you can review by commit.  There are two changes here - 

- validate dimensions that are attached to columns as part of the UnparsedColumn.  (This is necessary because the column holds the granularity value in this case.)
- for dimensions in derived_semantics, we add a new validation override that shows that the dimension contains a granularity when it has validity params.

title: Add parser support for `dbt.config.meta_get()` in Python models
body:
Resolves #

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

Updates the Python model parser to recognize and track `dbt.config.meta_get()` calls, extracting keys and default values for runtime use.

**Note**: Python models only support `meta_get()`, not `meta_require()` (unlike SQL models which have both).

#### What Changed
- **Parser Updates**: Modified `models.py` to recognize `dbt.meta_get()` function calls
- **Key Tracking**: Extracts meta keys and default values from `meta_get()` calls
- **Validation**: Validates argument count (1-2 args required)
- **Context Passing**: Passes `meta_keys_used` and `meta_keys_defaults` to runtime macro
- **Tests**: Added 6 unit tests covering various meta_get scenarios

#### Backwards Compatibility
- Fully backwards compatible - parser only tracks new function, doesn't break existing code
- Old pattern (`dbt.config.get('meta_key')`) continues to be parsed (though it won't work at runtime)

#### Related PRs
- dbt-adapters PR: Runtime macro that uses parsed meta keys
- Fusion PR: Deprecation warnings for old pattern
- dbt-autofix PR: Automatic migration tool

title: Restructure warn_error_options testing for better isolation
body:
Resolves #N/A

### Problem

We've been fighting our CI for a few days now. We were seeing slowness on of our runners after the recent github incidents related to action runners. Because of that slowness on our runners, our jobs were timing out. To fix that, we increased the concurrency of our tests (splitting our test suite into more parts handled by separate runners). However, in doing so, some flakiness in our test suite was uncovered, most persistently with our `warn_error_options` testing.

### Solution

Refactor our warn error options tests to be more in line with our typical testing integration/functional testing structure
1. Fixtures shouldn't be at the top level, but in `Base` test classes that are then inherited
2. Only one test per test class
  a. This is because dbt writes to the operating system. Multiple tests in the same class means they share an operating system, meaning tests can possibly clobber one another / become dependent on one another

### Checklist

- [X] I have read [the contributing guide](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and understand what's expected of me.
- [X] I have run this code in development, and it appears to resolve the stated issue.
- [X] This PR includes tests, or tests are not required or relevant for this PR.
- [X] This PR has no interface changes (e.g., macros, CLI, logs, JSON artifacts, config files, adapter interface, etc.) or this PR has already received feedback and approval from Product or DX.
- [X] This PR includes [type annotations](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html) for new and modified functions.
author: QMalcolm

commits.message:
Move fixtures for warn error options tests into base class

We don't typically declare fixtures at the top level of a testing file.
Doing so can lead to weirdness when those fixtures conflict with other
fixtures defined elsewhere. Additionally it can lead to situations where
the fixture is unintentionally reused.

Refactor warn error options tests to have one test per test class

A best practice generally in our functional/integration tests is to have
only one test per test class. This is because running dbt writes files
to the operating system. When multiple tests are in the same test class
they share an operating system meaning that the test can accidentally become
dependent on one another.

title: Fix FK constraint ref() resolving to wrong node during deferral
body:
## Summary

Fixes #12455

During deferral with `--defer`, foreign key constraint `ref()` was always resolving to the deferred (production) relation, even when the referenced model was being built as part of the current selection. This caused FK constraints to reference the wrong schema.

**Before (bug):**
```sql
-- bar is built in CI schema
create or replace db.ci.bar as (...)

-- foo's FK constraint incorrectly points to prod
create or replace db.ci.foo (
  fk int references db.prod.bar (id)  -- WRONG!
) as (
  select * from db.ci.bar  -- body ref is correct
)
```

**After (fix):**
```sql
-- bar is built in CI schema
create or replace db.ci.bar as (...)

-- foo's FK constraint correctly points to CI
create or replace db.ci.foo (
  fk int references db.ci.bar (id)  -- CORRECT!
) as (
  select * from db.ci.bar
)
```

## Changes

- Added `selected_node_ids` attribute to `Compiler` class to track nodes selected for the current run
- Modified `_compile_relation_for_foreign_key_constraint_to()` to use deferred relation **only if** FK target is NOT in selection
- Propagate selection info from task compiler to runner compilers
- Updated tests to verify both scenarios (target selected vs not selected)

This mirrors the behavior of `RuntimeRefResolver.create_relation()` for model body refs, ensuring FK constraints behave consistently.

## Test plan

- [x] Added tests for FK target NOT selected → uses deferred relation
- [x] Added tests for FK target IS selected → uses current relation  
- [x] All 12 FK constraint tests pass
- [x] Unit tests pass

## Disclaimer

This fix was developed mostly with the assistance of Claude Code.
author: b-per

commits.message:
Fix FK constraint ref() resolving to deferred relation when target is selected

During deferral, foreign key constraint ref() was always resolving to the
deferred (production) relation even when the referenced model was being
built as part of the current selection. This caused FK constraints to
reference the wrong schema.

The fix adds selection awareness to FK constraint compilation:
- Track selected node IDs on the Compiler instance
- Use deferred relation only if FK target is NOT in selection
- Propagate selection info from task to runner compilers

This mirrors the behavior of RuntimeRefResolver.create_relation() for
model body refs, ensuring FK constraints behave consistently.

Fixes #12455

title: Fix partial parsing duplicate for v2 inline semantic models
body:
# Why

When using the v2 inline semantic model format (`semantic_model: true` on a model entry with column-level `dimension`/`entity` fields), making any change to the model's YAML entry causes a false \"duplicate semantic model\" compilation error where both paths point to the identical file. The only workaround is `dbt clean && dbt parse --no-partial-parse`.

**Root cause:** `_delete_schema_mssa_links` in `partial.py` cleans up the model node and its tests, but has no awareness of v2 semantic models. These SMs are created as a side effect of `patch_node_properties` in `schemas.py` and are stored in `schema_file.semantic_models` / `saved_manifest.semantic_models`. On re-parse, `patch_node_properties` creates a new SM while the old one remains → duplicate.

# What

- Add `_delete_v2_semantic_model_for_model` method to `PartialParsing` that finds and removes any v2 inline SM (and associated auto-generated metrics) for a given model entry, by matching `sm.model == \"ref('<model_name>')\"` in the saved manifest.

- Call it from `_delete_schema_mssa_links` when `dict_key == \"models\"` — covers both the `changed` and `deleted` paths.

- Guard against accidentally removing v1 standalone SMs (under `semantic_models:` key) by checking `v1_sm_names` from `dict_from_yaml`.

- Add two regression tests:
  - `TestV2SemanticModelPartialParsingChanged` — modifies a column label and verifies exactly 1 SM remains after partial parse (not 2)
  - `TestV2SemanticModelPartialParsingDisabled` — changes `semantic_model: true` to `false` and verifies the SM is removed

# Refs

- Jira: DI-3697 (child of DI-3280 Semantic Layer Bugs)
- Customer impact: https://dbt-labs.slack.com/archives/C03KHQRQUBX/p1774983275570169

Drafted by claude-sonnet-4-6 under the direction of @theyostalservice
author: theyostalservice

reviews.body: Looks good to me, thank you for doing this work

commits.message:
Fix partial parsing duplicate for v2 inline semantic models (DI-3697)

When a model YAML entry with semantic_model: true was changed or disabled,
_delete_schema_mssa_links did not clean up the v2 semantic model that was
created as a side effect of model patching. On re-parse, patch_node_properties
created a new SM while the old one remained, causing a duplicate error.

Fix: add _delete_v2_semantic_model_for_model which looks up the saved manifest
for any v2 SM whose model ref matches the changed model, and removes it before
re-patching. Uses sm.model == "ref('<name>')" to identify v2 SMs and guards
against removing v1 SMs that are handled by the semantic_models diff path.

title: Improve error message for unknown fields in semantic_model config
body:
# Why

When a user adds an unsupported field inside the \`semantic_model:\` config object (e.g. \`description:\`, which the docs incorrectly showed as valid), dbt produces an unhelpful error:

\`\`\`
at path ['semantic_model']: {'enabled': True, 'name': 'purchases', 'description': '...'} is not valid under any of the given schemas
\`\`\`

The \`anyOf\` JSON Schema failure gives no indication of *which* field is wrong or what is allowed.

# What

- \`UnparsedSemanticModelConfig.validate()\` — checks for unknown fields and raises \`ValidationError\` with a message naming the offending field(s) and listing the valid ones
- \`UnparsedModelUpdate.validate()\` — calls the nested validator before the full JSON Schema so the clear error fires first

After this change the error reads:

\`\`\`
Unknown field(s) in semantic_model config: description. Valid fields are: config, enabled, group, name.
\`\`\`

**Tests:**
- \`tests/unit/utils/__init__.py\`: adds \`assert_fails_validation_with_message()\` helper to \`ContractTestCase\` for asserting both that validation fails and that the error message is actionable — intended for reuse as we improve errors on other unparsed objects
- \`tests/unit/contracts/graph/test_unparsed.py\`: adds \`TestUnparsedSemanticModelConfig\` with four unit tests covering valid config, single extra field, message lists valid fields, and multiple extra fields

# Refs

- Slack thread: C03KHQRQUBX / 1774638031.550019
- Related docs fix: #12765

Drafted by claude-sonnet-4-6 under the direction of @theyostalservice
author: theyostalservice

reviews.body: LGTM! Thank you for doing this work!

Improve error message for unknown fields in semantic_model config

When a user adds an unknown field (e.g. description) inside the
semantic_model: config object, replace the opaque JSON Schema error
"is not valid under any of the given schemas" with a clear message
naming the offending field and listing the valid ones.

title: fix: include exception message in run_results.json for run-operation failures
body:
## Problem

Fixes #8278

When `dbt run-operation` fails, the `run_results.json` artifact has `"message": null` even though the exception was captured and logged via `fire_event`. Pipeline orchestrators that parse `run_results.json` to get invocation results have to also parse log output as a workaround.

Other dbt commands (run, test, build) populate the `message` field on failure. `run-operation` is the exception.

## Fix

Capture the exception string in both the `DbtBaseException` and generic `Exception` handlers, then pass it to the `RunResult` constructor's `message` field instead of hardcoded `None`.

**Before:**
```json
{"status": "error", "message": null, "failures": 1}
```

**After:**
```json
{"status": "error", "message": "Database Error in macro my_macro...", "failures": 1}
```

4 lines added, 1 changed. The `message` field is `None` on success (no change to happy path behavior).
author: claygeo

commits.message:
fix: include exception message in run_results.json for run-operation failures

When dbt run-operation fails, the run_results.json artifact has
message: null even though the exception message was captured and
logged. Pipeline orchestrators parsing run_results.json have no way
to get the error message without also parsing log output.

Capture the exception string from both DbtBaseException and generic
Exception handlers and pass it to the RunResult message field.

Before: {"status": "error", "message": null, ...}
After:  {"status": "error", "message": "Database Error ...", ...}

Fixes #8278

Merge this first 👉 https://github.com/dbt-labs/iglu.sinter-collect.com/pull/100

reviews.body:
Initial thought here was that we we should consider puttings `catalogs` on the manifest object directly instead of the new plumbing through Task objects introduced here. Especially as we just coupled manifest loading with catalog loading in https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/pull/12705 given:

> The underlying invariant is that any command loading a manifest expects catalog context to be present. Models that use catalog_name or rely on catalog-level configs with model-level overrides need catalog integration to be registered before the manifest is parsed in order for those configurations to resolve accurately.

title: Part 1: Replace Python Exceptions with DbtException subclasses
body:
Resolves #

<!---
  Include the number of the issue addressed by this PR above, if applicable.
  PRs for code changes without an associated issue *will not be merged*.
  See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

  Add the `user docs` label to this PR if it will need docs changes.  An 
  issue will get opened in docs.getdbt.com upon successful merge of this PR.
-->

### Problem

<!---
  Describe the problem this PR is solving. What is the application state
  before this PR is merged?
-->

When dbt raises built-in exceptions that don't derive from `DbtBaseException`, dbt's exception handling treats this as unhandled and displays a full stacktrace. This makes it difficult to distinguish user issues from internal unhandled exceptions, as well as clutters up the stdout when it is meant to be a user exception for users to take action on.

Prior one-off example + fix for cycle detection: https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/pull/12684#issuecomment-4100464375

### Solution

<!---
  Describe the way this PR solves the above problem. Add as much detail as you
  can to help reviewers understand your changes. Include any alternatives and
  tradeoffs you considered.
-->

Replace built-in exceptions with the appropriate DbtException subclasses throughout the codebase. Each replacement was chosen to reflect the actual nature of the failure:

* Exception → DbtInternalError                                                                                                                          
    * core/dbt/cli/flags.py — deprecated param name not found in DEPRECATED_PARAMS or command context                          
     * core/dbt/parser/partial.py — serialization failure for a schema file; missing parse_file_type/project_name on a
  SourceFile; invalid parse_file_type during partial parsing                                                                 
           
* ValueError → DbtInternalError                                                                                                    
    * core/dbt/artifacts/schemas/base.py — artifact missing dbt_schema_version in metadata
    * core/dbt/parser/schema_yaml_readers.py — invalid semantic model config (explicitly marked unreachable)
                                                                                                                             
* RuntimeError → DbtInternalError
  * core/dbt/deprecations.py — unknown deprecation warning name passed to warn()                                             
   
* RuntimeError → DbtRuntimeError                                                                                                      
  - core/dbt/clients/git.py — installed git version too old to support sparse checkout (< 2.25.0)

* RuntimeError → ParsingError
  - core/dbt/contracts/files.py — invalid language specified for a RemoteFile                                                
   
* ValueError → CompilationError                                                                                              
  * core/dbt/graph/graph.py — model not present in graph after selection (disabled or missing)           


Mashumaro validation errors will be a follow-up!

- [x] I have read [the contributing guide](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and understand what's expected of me.

commits.message:
Replace built-in RuntimeError with DbtException subclasses

  Replace raw RuntimeError raises with appropriate DbtException subclasses
  so these errors are handled consistently within dbt's exception hierarchy.
  - contracts/files.py: invalid language → ParsingError
  - clients/git.py: old git version → DbtRuntimeError
  - deprecations.py: unknown deprecation name → DbtRuntimeError
  - task/printer.py: unhandled result status → DbtRuntimeError

  Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Replace ValueError with DbtException subclasses

  - artifacts/schemas/base.py: missing schema version → DbtInternalError
  - graph/graph.py: model not found in graph → CompilationError
  - Update test_retry.py to expect CompilationError

  Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
manually fix a few errant exception categories

Replace ValueError with DbtInternalError in schema_yaml_readers

  Unreachable branch guarding invalid semantic model config type
  should raise a DbtInternalError rather than a plain ValueError.

  Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Replace bare Exception with DbtInternalError in flags and partial parser

  These are all internal invariant violations that should never be reached
  in normal operation.

  Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
